meta.title: 4.1 The Three Layers
meta.description: Understand the DOM and trace how a user interaction flows through JavaScript, HTML, and CSS.

blocks.prompt: When you click a button on a webpage and something changes visually — say a menu slides open — what do you think is happening behind the scenes? Which of the three languages (HTML, CSS, JS) do you think is involved, and in what order?

blocks.question: On a scale of 1-5, how confident are you that you could look at a button click interaction and trace which part is HTML, which is CSS, and which is JavaScript?
blocks.answer: If you rated yourself 3 or higher, you are in good shape. The key insight is the flow: JS finds the element, changes a class, and CSS rules for that class handle the visual result. If you are below 3, re-read the sidebar example above — that one pattern covers most interactive behavior on the web.

options: A) Click → JS adds 'visible' class → CSS rule `.dropdown.visible` applies → dropdown appears
B) Click → CSS detects the click → dropdown appears → JS records the change
C) Click → HTML changes the class → CSS applies → JS logs the event
problems.explanation: JavaScript handles the click event and adds the 'visible' class to the dropdown element. The browser then sees that .dropdown.visible matches, applies display: block, and the dropdown becomes visible. CSS does not detect clicks — that is JavaScript's job.

problems.question: A developer wrote JavaScript that adds the class `active` to a button when clicked. The class gets added (you can see it in DevTools), but the button does not change appearance. What is the most likely problem?
options: A) JavaScript is adding the wrong class name
B) There is no CSS rule for the `.active` class, so nothing visual happens
C) The button's HTML is missing a `style` attribute
problems.explanation: If DevTools shows the class being added successfully, JavaScript is doing its job. The problem is in CSS — there is no rule that says what .active should look like. Adding a CSS rule like .active { background-color: blue; } would fix it.

problems.question: A contact form has a "Submit" button that is supposed to send data when clicked. The button appears on the page and is styled correctly, but clicking it does nothing at all. Which layer is most likely the problem?
options: A) HTML — the button is probably missing from the page
B) CSS — the button's styles are preventing the click
C) JavaScript — there is no event listener attached to the button
problems.explanation: The button is visible (HTML is fine) and looks correct (CSS is fine). If clicking does nothing, JavaScript is the most likely culprit — either there is no event listener for the click, or the listener has an error that prevents it from running.

blocks.prompt: In your own words, explain the flow of what happens when a user clicks a button and something changes visually on a webpage. Walk through each layer's role.

blocks.content: The DOM is the browser's live representation of the page. JavaScript modifies the DOM (often by adding or removing CSS classes), and CSS rules respond to those changes with visual updates. The flow is: User action → JS event → DOM change → CSS applies → Visual update. When something breaks, one of those links in the chain is missing.

blocks.prompt: Now you understand how the three layers connect in theory. But how do you actually see this happening on a real page? Next, we will look at the most powerful tool non-developers have for understanding webpages: browser DevTools.

reviewQuestions.question: The DOM (Document Object Model) is:
options: The raw HTML file saved on disk
The browser's live, in-memory representation of the page that JavaScript can modify
reviewQuestions.explanation: The DOM is the live version of the page in the browser's memory. JavaScript modifies the DOM, not the original HTML file.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Exactly! The DOM is the live page — JavaScript changes the DOM, not the file.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The DOM is the browser's live representation of the page. JavaScript modifies this live version, not the original HTML file.

reviewQuestions.question: The typical interaction flow on a webpage is:
options: CSS event -> HTML update -> JS render
User action -> JS event -> DOM change -> CSS applies -> Visual update
HTML loads -> Page is static forever
reviewQuestions.explanation: A user does something -> JavaScript listens for the event -> JS modifies the DOM -> CSS rules respond to the new state -> the user sees the change.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the flow! User action, JS event, DOM change, CSS responds, visual update.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The interaction flow: User action -> JavaScript event fires -> DOM is modified -> CSS applies to new state -> User sees the visual change.
